records.ety: [OE. bundel, AS. byndel; akin to D. bondel, bundel, G. bündel, dim. of bund bundle, fr. the root of E. bind. See Bind.]

defs: A number of things bound together, as by a cord or envelope, into a mass or package convenient for handling or conveyance; a loose package; a roll; as, a bundle of straw or of paper; a bundle of old clothes.
quotes: The fable of the rods, which, when united in a bundle, no strength could bend.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Bundled (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Bundling (<?/).]

defs: To tie or bind in a bundle or roll.
To send off abruptly or without ceremony.
quotes: They unmercifully bundled me and my gallant second into our own hackney coach.

defs: to sell together as a single item at one inclusive price; -- usually done for related products which work or are used together.
To prepare for departure; to set off in a hurry or without ceremony.
To sleep on the same bed without undressing; -- applied to the custom of a man and woman, especially lovers, thus sleeping.
quotes: Van Corlear stopped occasionally in the villages to eat pumpkin pies, dance at country frolics, and bundle with the Yankee lasses.
